{{Shrine | name = Osaka Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Osaka-gokoku-jinja1.jpg|250px|Osaka Gokoku Shrine]] | location = [[Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]], [[Osaka City|Osaka]], [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Minamikagaya]] 1-1-77 | deities = Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture | rank = Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Shrine, [[Bekkaku Shrine|Bekkaku Shrine]] | founded = 1940 (Showa 15) | annual_festival = [[May 20|May 20 (Spring)]] ・ [[October 20|October 20 (Autumn)]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|36|36.5|N|135|28|26.1|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Osaka Gokoku Shrine''' (おおさかごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Osaka]], Japan. It enshrines over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture, making it the third largest Gokoku Shrine in terms of the number of enshrined deities, following [[Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]] and [[Fukuoka Gokoku Shrine]]. The shrine is a [[Bekkaku Shrine|Bekkaku Shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
The shrine is located southwest of [[Suminoe Park]] and adjacent to the [[Suminoe Boat Racecourse]]. The main torii gate, which faces [[Suminoe Avenue]], is the largest in Osaka Prefecture.
== History == The first attempt to enshrine Osaka’s war dead was made on April 10, 1871 (Meiji 4), with the creation of a [[Shōkonsha]] within the [[Sanadayama Army Cemetery]] grounds. However, after the [[Satsuma Rebellion]], memorial services were held at various locations, including [[Osaka Castle]] and the monument to Emperor Meiji at [[Nakanoshima]], before finally moving to [[Jōtō Drill Ground]] in 1900 (Meiji 33). The original Shōkonsha at Sanadayama was abolished by 1918 (Taishō 7).
Due to the growing reverence for war dead following the outbreak of the [[Second Sino-Japanese War]], the Governor of Osaka established the Gokoku Shrine Construction Support Association in 1938 (Shōwa 13). The site, a low-lying area southwest of Suminoe Park, was filled in by about 360,000 volunteers over two years. The shrine was designated a Gokoku Shrine by the Interior Minister and was enshrined on May 4, 1940 (Shōwa 15), initially in temporary buildings due to shortages of materials and labor caused by the war.
After World War II, the shrine was renamed "Naniwa-no-Miya" and enshrined Emperor Nintoku in response to the GHQ’s Shinto Directive. The shrine was restored to its original name, Osaka Gokoku Shrine, following the signing of the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952 (Shōwa 27).
In 1960 (Shōwa 35), a new shrine building was constructed, and the shrine was officially moved to its current location in 1963 (Shōwa 38). The shrine was visited by Emperor Shōwa and Empress Kōjun on July 15, 1970, and by the Crown Prince and Princess (now Emperor Emeritus Akihito and Empress Emerita Michiko) on May 31, 1978.
A statue honoring special attack forces was erected on October 24, 2009 (Heisei 21).
== Deities == * Main Deity - Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture (exactly 105,665)
== Clergy and Activities == * Former chief priest Chūmaro Yanagisawa served as the Osaka executive chairman of [[Nippon Kaigi]] and the Osaka prefectural head of the [[Shinto Seiji Renmei]]. He passed away on March 31, 2018. * Masachika Fujie became chief priest in 2021 (Reiwa 3). He organized a memorial service for former Prime Minister [[Shinzo Abe]] following his assassination.
== Grounds == * Main Hall - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Heiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Haiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Hōanden - Enshrines [[Emperor Nintoku]] and Admiral [[Tōgō Heihachirō]] * Sorei-sha "Homare-no-Miya" * Festival Warehouses * Administrative Offices * Suminoe Hall * Ritual Hall (Former Gokoku Dojo) * Garden * Memorials for various military units
== Access == * Near [[Suminoe Koen Station]] on the [[Osaka Metro Yotsubashi Line]] and [[Osaka Metro Nanko Port Town Line|New Tram]]

{{神社 |名称 = 大阪護國神社 |画像 = [[画像:Osaka-gokoku-jinja1.jpg|250px|大阪護國神社]]
鳥居と拝殿 |所在地 = [[大阪府]][[大阪市]][[住之江区]][[南加賀屋]]1丁目1-77 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|36|36.5|N|135|28|26.1|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = 大阪府出身ならびに縁故の殉国の[[英霊]]10万5千余[[柱]] |社格 = [[内務大臣 (日本)|内務大臣]]指定護国神社・[[別表神社]] |創建 = [[1940年]]（[[昭和]]15年） |例祭 = [[5月20日]]（春季）・[[10月20日]]（秋季） |地図={{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=大阪護國神社|coordinates= {{coord2|34|36|36.5|N|135|28|26.1|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|36|36.5|N|135|28|26.1|E}}}} }} '''大阪護國神社'''（おおさかごこくじんじゃ）は、[[大阪市]][[住之江区]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）。大阪府出身ならびに縁故の殉国の[[英霊]]10万5千余[[柱]]を祀っており、祭神の柱数は護国神社では[[沖縄県護国神社]]、[[福岡縣護國神社]]に次いで3番目に多い。現在は[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]。 [[住之江公園]]の南西に鎮座し、[[新なにわ筋]]を挟んで[[住之江競艇場]]に隣接している。[[住之江通]]に面した正面[[鳥居]]は大阪府で最大の鳥居である。 == 歴史 == 大阪府では、[[1871年]]（[[明治]]4年）[[4月10日]]に創設された[[真田山陸軍墓地]]の敷地内に[[招魂社]]が創建されたのが最初である。しかし、招魂祭は、[[西南戦争]]後は[[大日本帝国陸軍|陸軍]]の拠点である[[大坂城]]内で、[[1883年]]（明治16年）以降は[[中之島 (大阪府)|中之島]]に建立された明治紀念標{{efn|明治紀念標は[[1902年]]（明治35年）に[[大手前]]の大阪[[偕行社]]敷地内に移設され、[[1943年]]（[[昭和]]18年）に供出されており、現存しない。}}前で、[[1900年]]（明治33年）以降は城東練兵場{{efn|現在の大阪市[[城東区]][[森之宮]]。[[大阪砲兵工廠]]の拡張に伴い、[[1940年]]（昭和15年）に[[中河内郡]][[盾津|盾津町]]へ移転した。}}で行われていた。なお、正確な時期は不明であるが、真田山の招魂社は[[1918年]]（[[大正]]7年）までには廃絶されている。 招魂祭が長らく社前で行われなくなっていたことや、[[支那事変]]（[[日中戦争]]）勃発後に[[英霊]]崇敬の機運が高まりを見せたことから、[[1938年]]（[[昭和]]13年）に大阪府[[知事]]が奉賛会長として護国神社造営奉賛会を結成し、創建予定地である[[住之江公園]]の南西の低湿地を、府民のべ約36万人が勤労奉仕で約2年かけて埋め立てて、[[内務大臣 (日本)|内務大臣]]指定護國神社として創建された{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.osakagokoku.or.jp/publics/index/80/ |title=御祭神・創建・沿革 |publisher=大阪護國神社 |accessdate=2019-01-07}}。[[1940年]]（昭和15年）[[5月4日]]に鎮座祭が行われたが、人材・資材不足のため正式な社殿の建築をすることができず、仮社殿での鎮座であった。その後に正式な社殿の造営を行う予定であったが、[[大東亜戦争]]の激化や敗戦後の混乱により着工出来ないままであった。 戦後、[[連合国軍最高司令官総司令部|GHQ]]による[[神道指令]]の影響下における存続対策として[[仁徳天皇]]を祀り、「浪速宮（なにわぐう）」と称していた{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.osakagokoku.or.jp/publics/index/7/ |title=ご由緒 |publisher=大阪護國神社 |accessdate=2019-01-07}}。また、[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]に加列されている。 社名は、[[サンフランシスコ講和条約]]締結後の[[1952年]]（昭和27年）に大阪護國神社に復称している{{R|ご由緒}}。こうした経緯から奉安殿に[[仁徳天皇]]を、その相殿に[[東郷平八郎]]を遺髪と共に祀るようになった。 [[1960年]]（昭和35年）に造営奉賛会が結成され、[[1963年]]（昭和38年）春に社殿が竣工し、[[5月29日]]に遷座祭が行われた{{R|沿革}}。[[1970年]]（昭和45年）[[7月15日]]に[[昭和天皇]]と[[香淳皇后]]が親拝され、[[1978年]]（昭和53年）[[5月31日]]には[[皇太子]]・[[皇太子妃]]（現：[[明仁]][[上皇 (天皇退位特例法)|上皇]]・[[上皇后美智子]]）が参拝されている。 [[2009年]]（[[平成]]21年）[[10月24日]]に[[特攻勇士の像]]が建立された。 == 祭神 == * 主祭神 - 大阪府出身ならびに縁故の殉国の[[英霊]]10万5千余[[柱]]（正確には105,665柱）[https://spicomi.net/media/articles/3176 大阪護國神社の徹底取材レポ！ご利益・御朱印・お守り] == 神官・諸活動 == * 元宮司の柳澤忠麿は[[日本会議]]大阪運営委員長、[[神道政治連盟]]大阪府本部長を務めていた{{cite web | url=http://shinseiren-kyoto.jp/wp/wp-content/uploads/2023/06/seisei35.pdf | title=『清政』35号 | publisher=神道政治連盟京都府本部 | date=2003-12-16 | accessdate=2024-3-4 }}。2018年（平成30年）3月31日、柳澤は死去した{{cite web | url=http://kaireikai.main.jp/kokubetsu2018.04.03.htm | title=故　柳澤忠麿　海励会会長 通夜式・告別式　(2018年4月2、3日) | publisher=海上自衛隊を励ます会 | date= | accessdate=2024-3-4 }}。 * 2018年（平成30年）、柳澤の死去に伴い、日本会議大阪南河内支部長の南坊城充興が宮司に就任した{{cite web | url=https://www.nipponkaigi.osaka/?p=3079 | title=日本の息吹　［大阪版］　～ 一人がひとりを！ ～　　第１７8号　平成26年9月号 | publisher=日本会議大阪 | date=2014-8-25 | accessdate=2024-3-4 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.nipponkaigi.osaka/?p=4825 | title=日本の息吹［大阪版］～一人がひとりを！～ 第22３号 平成３０年１０月号 | publisher=日本会議大阪 | date=2018-9-24 | accessdate=2024-3-4 }}。 * 2021年（令和3年）4月、藤江正鎮が宮司に就任。2022年（令和4年）9月中旬、共同通信やNHKなど8つの報道機関が実施した[[故安倍晋三国葬儀|安倍晋三元首相の国葬]]に関する世論調査・アンケート調査ではいずれも「反対」が「賛成」を上回った{{Cite web|和書|title=世論調査（共同通信データ） |publisher=[[東京新聞]] |date=2022-09-22 |url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/digican/shuin2021_yoron?sjkd_page=cont_KA613b0d7b468bd_KA632bf2819c8e9 |access-date=2022-09-24}}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nhk.or.jp/politics/articles/lastweek/89020.html |title=国葬「評価しない」57％ 旧統一教会への対応は？ NHK世論調査 |access-date=2022-09-30 |publisher=日本放送協会 |date=2022-09-13 |website=NHK政治マガジン |language=ja}}{{Cite news|title=安倍元首相の国葬賛否、反対56%　8月比で賛成減る　朝日世論調査|newspaper=朝日新聞デジタル|date=2022-09-12|url=https://digital.asahi.com/articles/ASQ9D3GNCQ9DUZPS002.html|access-date=2022-09-16|publisher=[[朝日新聞社]]}}。これを受けて藤江は[[自由民主党 (日本)|自由民主党]]大阪府連などに問い合わせ、府内に弔意を示す場がないことを知った。9月26日、大阪護國神社藤江は公式Twitterに献花台を翌日に置くことを通知。Twitterで寄せられた「いいね」は2万件を超えた。同月27日、藤江は産経新聞の取材に応じ、「国葬反対の声ばかりが取り上げられているが、『あれだけ功績があるのに』という憤りがあった。（多くの弔問に）安倍さんが喜んでくれているとうれしい」と述べた{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.yomiuri.co.jp/national/20220927-OYT1T50157/|title=『大阪護国神社にも献花台、多くの人が花手向ける…涙流す人も』|publisher=讀賣新聞オンライン|date=2022-09-27 |accessdate=2023-02-11}}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.iza.ne.jp/article/20220927-TWSYXKZLJRK35GW55ILMMXFUAM/|title=『大阪護国神社に献花台「感謝伝えたい」ツイッターで反響も』|publisher=産経新聞オンライン|date=2022-09-27 |accessdate=2023-02-11}}{{cite web | author= | url=https://www.sankei.com/article/20230328-LNLWIY45RJIKZDSJPRFEVMJUJE/ | title=【浪速風】暴力で失った無念 | publisher=産経新聞 | date=2023-3-28 | accessdate=2024-3-4 }}。 * 2023年（令和5年）3月26日、板垣退助先生顕彰会が主催する「安倍晋三元総理を偲ぶ慰霊祭」の会場となった。 == 境内 == * 本殿 - [[1963年]]（[[昭和]]38年）春建立。 * 幣殿 - 1963年（昭和38年）春建立。[[神鏡]]は戦前の研磨技術で作ることが出来た最大のものとされており、当時三面作られ、[[東京都]][[渋谷区]]・[[明治神宮]]、[[兵庫県]][[神戸市]]・[[湊川神社]]と当社の三社に奉納されたと伝わっている。 * 拝殿 - 1963年（昭和38年）春建立。 * 奉安殿 - 祭神：[[仁徳天皇]]、[[東郷平八郎]][[元帥]] * 祖霊社「ほまれの宮」 * 祭器庫 * 社務所 * 住之江会館 * 儀式殿（元護國道場） * 庭園 * [[サイパン島]]戦没者慰霊碑 * [[歩兵第217連隊|歩兵第二百十七連隊]]之碑 * [[野砲兵第26連隊|野砲兵第二十六連隊]] つわもの之碑 * [[大日本帝国海軍|海軍]]第一期 飛行専修予備生徒慰霊碑 * 独立輜重兵 第五十四大隊慰霊碑 * [[歩兵第216連隊|歩兵第二百十六連隊]]鎮魂碑 * 香8114部隊（独立歩兵第六十七大隊） 殉國の英霊 このところに鎮まる之碑 * [[歩兵第108連隊|歩兵第百八連隊]] つわもの之霊 ここに鎮まる之碑 * [[歩兵第79連隊|歩兵第七十九連隊]] 戦友はここに眠る之碑 * [[信太山演習場|信太山]][[野砲兵第4連隊|砲四]]会 鎮魂之碑 * 関西甲飛会 [[海軍飛行予科練習生|予科練]]貴様と俺と翼之碑 * [[第34師団 (日本軍)|第三十四師団]]通信隊 鎮魂之碑 * [[騎兵連隊|騎]][[捜索連隊|捜]]会 鎮魂之碑 * 海軍関係戦没者慰霊之碑 * [[第68師団 (日本軍)|第六十八師団]]（檜会） 慰霊碑 * [[歩兵第14連隊|歩兵第十四連隊]]之慰霊碑 * 平和の礎 [[歩兵第8連隊|歩兵第八連隊]]之慰霊碑 * [[歩兵第37連隊|歩兵第三十七連隊]]慰霊碑 * [[騎兵連隊|騎兵第四連隊]]慰霊碑 * 海軍[[大尉]] 粟井俊夫之碑 * 皇風洽六合 明徳侔太陽之碑（海軍[[大将]][[塩沢幸一]]題） * 母に感謝の像 * [[特攻勇士の像]] - [[2009年]]（[[平成]]21年）10月24日造。 * [[安倍晋三]]元総理を偲ぶ[[向日葵]]の栽培 - [[2023年]]（[[令和]]5年）造{{efn|[[2022年]]（[[令和]]4年）9月に行われた[[安倍晋三]]元総理を偲ぶ「[[国葬]]」において寄せられた献花を堆肥として栽培された[[向日葵]]。（境内の説明板による）}}。 == 交通 == * [[Osaka Metro四つ橋線]]・[[Osaka Metro南港ポートタウン線|ニュートラム]] [[住之江公園駅]]下車すぐ == revision.text:
{{Shrine | name = Osaka Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Osaka-gokoku-jinja1.jpg|250px|Osaka Gokoku Shrine]] | location = [[Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]], [[Osaka City|Osaka]], [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Minamikagaya]] 1-1-77 | deities = Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture | rank = Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Shrine, [[Bekkaku Shrine|Bekkaku Shrine]] | founded = 1940 (Showa 15) | annual_festival = [[May 20|May 20 (Spring)]] ・ [[October 20|October 20 (Autumn)]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|36|36.5|N|135|28|26.1|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Osaka Gokoku Shrine''' (おおさかごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Osaka]], Japan. It enshrines over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture, making it the third largest Gokoku Shrine in terms of the number of enshrined deities, following [[Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]] and [[Fukuoka Gokoku Shrine]]. The shrine is a [[Bekkaku Shrine|Bekkaku Shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
The shrine is located southwest of [[Suminoe Park]] and adjacent to the [[Suminoe Boat Racecourse]]. The main torii gate, which faces [[Suminoe Avenue]], is the largest in Osaka Prefecture.
== History == The first attempt to enshrine Osaka’s war dead was made on April 10, 1871 (Meiji 4), with the creation of a [[Shōkonsha]] within the [[Sanadayama Army Cemetery]] grounds. However, after the [[Satsuma Rebellion]], memorial services were held at various locations, including [[Osaka Castle]] and the monument to Emperor Meiji at [[Nakanoshima]], before finally moving to [[Jōtō Drill Ground]] in 1900 (Meiji 33). The original Shōkonsha at Sanadayama was abolished by 1918 (Taishō 7).
Due to the growing reverence for war dead following the outbreak of the [[Second Sino-Japanese War]], the Governor of Osaka established the Gokoku Shrine Construction Support Association in 1938 (Shōwa 13). The site, a low-lying area southwest of Suminoe Park, was filled in by about 360,000 volunteers over two years. The shrine was designated a Gokoku Shrine by the Interior Minister and was enshrined on May 4, 1940 (Shōwa 15), initially in temporary buildings due to shortages of materials and labor caused by the war.
After World War II, the shrine was renamed "Naniwa-no-Miya" and enshrined Emperor Nintoku in response to the GHQ’s Shinto Directive. The shrine was restored to its original name, Osaka Gokoku Shrine, following the signing of the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952 (Shōwa 27).
In 1960 (Shōwa 35), a new shrine building was constructed, and the shrine was officially moved to its current location in 1963 (Shōwa 38). The shrine was visited by Emperor Shōwa and Empress Kōjun on July 15, 1970, and by the Crown Prince and Princess (now Emperor Emeritus Akihito and Empress Emerita Michiko) on May 31, 1978.
A statue honoring special attack forces was erected on October 24, 2009 (Heisei 21).
== Deities == * Main Deity - Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture (exactly 105,665)
== Clergy and Activities == * Former chief priest Chūmaro Yanagisawa served as the Osaka executive chairman of [[Nippon Kaigi]] and the Osaka prefectural head of the [[Shinto Seiji Renmei]]. He passed away on March 31, 2018. * Masachika Fujie became chief priest in 2021 (Reiwa 3). He organized a memorial service for former Prime Minister [[Shinzo Abe]] following his assassination.
== Grounds == * Main Hall - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Heiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Haiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Hōanden - Enshrines [[Emperor Nintoku]] and Admiral [[Tōgō Heihachirō]] * Sorei-sha "Homare-no-Miya" * Festival Warehouses * Administrative Offices * Suminoe Hall * Ritual Hall (Former Gokoku Dojo) * Garden * Memorials for various military units
== Access == * Near [[Suminoe Koen Station]] on the [[Osaka Metro Yotsubashi Line]] and [[Osaka Metro Nanko Port Town Line|New Tram]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == {{commonscat|Osaka-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Suminoe Park]]
== External Links == * [https://www.osakagokoku.or.jp/ Osaka Gokoku Shrine Official Website]

{{Shrine | name = Osaka Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Osaka-gokoku-jinja1.jpg|250px|Osaka Gokoku Shrine]] | location = [[Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]], [[Osaka City|Osaka]], [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Minamikagaya]] 1-1-77 | deities = Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture | rank = Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Shrine, [[Bekkaku Shrine|Bekkaku Shrine]] | founded = 1940 (Showa 15) | annual_festival = [[May 20|May 20 (Spring)]] ・ [[October 20|October 20 (Autumn)]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|36|36.5|N|135|28|26.1|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Osaka Gokoku Shrine''' (おおさかごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Osaka]], Japan. It enshrines over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture, making it the third largest Gokoku Shrine in terms of the number of enshrined deities, following [[Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]] and [[Fukuoka Gokoku Shrine]]. The shrine is a [[Bekkaku Shrine|Bekkaku Shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
The shrine is located southwest of [[Suminoe Park]] and adjacent to the [[Suminoe Boat Racecourse]]. The main torii gate, which faces [[Suminoe Avenue]], is the largest in Osaka Prefecture.
== History == The first attempt to enshrine Osaka’s war dead was made on April 10, 1871 (Meiji 4), with the creation of a [[Shōkonsha]] within the [[Sanadayama Army Cemetery]] grounds. However, after the [[Satsuma Rebellion]], memorial services were held at various locations, including [[Osaka Castle]] and the monument to Emperor Meiji at [[Nakanoshima]], before finally moving to [[Jōtō Drill Ground]] in 1900 (Meiji 33). The original Shōkonsha at Sanadayama was abolished by 1918 (Taishō 7).
Due to the growing reverence for war dead following the outbreak of the [[Second Sino-Japanese War]], the Governor of Osaka established the Gokoku Shrine Construction Support Association in 1938 (Shōwa 13). The site, a low-lying area southwest of Suminoe Park, was filled in by about 360,000 volunteers over two years. The shrine was designated a Gokoku Shrine by the Interior Minister and was enshrined on May 4, 1940 (Shōwa 15), initially in temporary buildings due to shortages of materials and labor caused by the war.
After World War II, the shrine was renamed "Naniwa-no-Miya" and enshrined Emperor Nintoku in response to the GHQ’s Shinto Directive. The shrine was restored to its original name, Osaka Gokoku Shrine, following the signing of the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952 (Shōwa 27).
In 1960 (Shōwa 35), a new shrine building was constructed, and the shrine was officially moved to its current location in 1963 (Shōwa 38). The shrine was visited by Emperor Shōwa and Empress Kōjun on July 15, 1970, and by the Crown Prince and Princess (now Emperor Emeritus Akihito and Empress Emerita Michiko) on May 31, 1978.
A statue honoring special attack forces was erected on October 24, 2009 (Heisei 21).
== Deities == * Main deity - Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture (exactly 105,665)
== Clergy and Activities == * Former chief priest Chūmaro Yanagisawa served as the Osaka executive chairman of [[Nippon Kaigi]] and the Osaka prefectural head of the [[Shinto Seiji Renmei]]. He passed away on March 31, 2018. * Masachika Fujie became chief priest in 2021 (Reiwa 3). He organized a memorial service for former Prime Minister [[Shinzo Abe]] following his assassination.
== Grounds == * Main Hall - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Heiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Haiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Hōanden - Enshrines [[Emperor Nintoku]] and Admiral [[Tōgō Heihachirō]] * Sorei-sha "Homare-no-Miya" * Festival Warehouses * Administrative Offices * Suminoe Hall * Ritual Hall (Former Gokoku Dojo) * Garden * Memorials for various military units
== Access == * Near [[Suminoe Koen Station]] on the [[Osaka Metro Yotsubashi Line]] and [[Osaka Metro Nanko Port Town Line|New Tram]]

'''Osaka Gokoku Shrine''' (おおさかごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Osaka]], Japan. It enshrines over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture, making it the third largest Gokoku Shrine in terms of the number of enshrined deities, following [[Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]] and [[Fukuoka Gokoku Shrine]]. The shrine is a [[Bekkaku Shrine|Bekkaku Shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
The shrine is located southwest of [[Suminoe Park]] and adjacent to the [[Suminoe Boat Racecourse]]. The main torii gate, which faces [[Suminoe Avenue]], is the largest in Osaka Prefecture.
== History == The first attempt to enshrine Osaka’s war dead was made on April 10, 1871 (Meiji 4), with the creation of a [[Shōkonsha]] within the [[Sanadayama Army Cemetery]] grounds. However, after the [[Satsuma Rebellion]], memorial services were held at various locations, including [[Osaka Castle]] and the monument to Emperor Meiji at [[Nakanoshima]], before finally moving to [[Jōtō Drill Ground]] in 1900 (Meiji 33). The original Shōkonsha at Sanadayama was abolished by 1918 (Taishō 7).
Due to the growing reverence for war dead following the outbreak of the [[Second Sino-Japanese War]], the Governor of Osaka established the Gokoku Shrine Construction Support Association in 1938 (Shōwa 13). The site, a low-lying area southwest of Suminoe Park, was filled in by about 360,000 volunteers over two years. The shrine was designated a Gokoku Shrine by the Interior Minister and was enshrined on May 4, 1940 (Shōwa 15), initially in temporary buildings due to shortages of materials and labor caused by the war.
After World War II, the shrine was renamed "Naniwa-no-Miya" and enshrined Emperor Nintoku in response to the GHQ’s Shinto Directive. The shrine was restored to its original name, Osaka Gokoku Shrine, following the signing of the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952 (Shōwa 27).
In 1960 (Shōwa 35), a new shrine building was constructed, and the shrine was officially moved to its current location in 1963 (Shōwa 38). The shrine was visited by Emperor Shōwa and Empress Kōjun on July 15, 1970, and by the Crown Prince and Princess (now Emperor Emeritus Akihito and Empress Emerita Michiko) on May 31, 1978.

The shrine is a [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
The shrine is located southwest of [[Suminoe Park]] and adjacent to the [[Suminoe Boat Racecourse]]. The main torii gate, which faces [[Suminoe Avenue]], is the largest in Osaka Prefecture.
== History == The first attempt to enshrine Osaka’s war dead was made on April 10, 1871 (Meiji 4), with the creation of a [[Shōkonsha]] within the [[Sanadayama Army Cemetery]] grounds. However, after the [[Satsuma Rebellion]], memorial services were held at various locations, including [[Osaka Castle]] and the monument to Emperor Meiji at [[Nakanoshima]], before finally moving to [[Jōtō Drill Ground]] in 1900 (Meiji 33). The original Shōkonsha at Sanadayama was abolished by 1918 (Taishō 7).
Due to the growing reverence for war dead following the outbreak of the [[Second Sino-Japanese War]], the Governor of Osaka established the Gokoku Shrine Construction Support Association in 1938 (Shōwa 13). The site, a low-lying area southwest of Suminoe Park, was filled in by about 360,000 volunteers over two years. The shrine was designated a Gokoku Shrine by the Interior Minister and was enshrined on May 4, 1940 (Shōwa 15), initially in temporary buildings due to shortages of materials and labor caused by the war.
After World War II, the shrine was renamed "Naniwa-no-Miya" and enshrined Emperor Nintoku in response to the GHQ’s Shinto Directive. The shrine was restored to its original name, Osaka Gokoku Shrine, following the signing of the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952 (Shōwa 27).
In 1960 (Shōwa 35), a new shrine building was constructed, and the shrine was officially moved to its current location in 1963 (Shōwa 38). The shrine was visited by Emperor Shōwa and Empress Kōjun on July 15, 1970, and by the Crown Prince and Princess (now Emperor Emeritus Akihito and Empress Emerita Michiko) on May 31, 1978.
A statue honoring special attack forces was erected on October 24, 2009 (Heisei 21).
== Deities == * Main deity - Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture (exactly 105,665)
== Clergy and Activities == * Former chief priest Chūmaro Yanagisawa served as the Osaka executive chairman of [[Nippon Kaigi]] and the Osaka prefectural head of the [[Shinto Seiji Renmei]]. He passed away on March 31, 2018. * Masachika Fujie became chief priest in 2021 (Reiwa 3). He organized a memorial service for former Prime Minister [[Shinzo Abe]] following his assassination.
== Grounds == * main hall - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Heiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Haiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Hōanden - Enshrines [[Emperor Nintoku]] and Admiral [[Tōgō Heihachirō]] * Sorei-sha "Homare-no-Miya" * festival Warehouses * Administrative Offices * Suminoe Hall * Ritual Hall (Former Gokoku Dojo) * Garden * Memorials for various military units
== Access == * Near [[Suminoe Koen Station]] on the [[Osaka Metro Yotsubashi Line]] and [[Osaka Metro Nanko Port Town Line|New Tram]]

'''Osaka Gokoku Shrine''' (おおさかごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Osaka]], Japan. It enshrines over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture, making it the third largest Gokoku Shrine in terms of the number of enshrined deities, following [[Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]] and [[Fukuoka Gokoku Shrine]]. The shrine is a [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
The shrine is located southwest of [[Suminoe Park]] and adjacent to the [[Suminoe Boat Racecourse]]. The main torii gate, which faces [[Suminoe Avenue]], is the largest in Osaka Prefecture.
== History == The first attempt to enshrine Osaka’s war dead was made on April 10, 1871 (Meiji 4), with the creation of a [[Shōkonsha]] within the [[Sanadayama Army Cemetery]] grounds. However, after the [[Satsuma Rebellion]], memorial services were held at various locations, including [[Osaka Castle]] and the monument to Emperor Meiji at [[Nakanoshima]], before finally moving to [[Jōtō Drill Ground]] in 1900 (Meiji 33). The original Shōkonsha at Sanadayama was abolished by 1918 (Taishō 7).
Due to the growing reverence for war dead following the outbreak of the [[Second Sino-Japanese War]], the Governor of Osaka established the Gokoku Shrine Construction Support Association in 1938 (Shōwa 13). The site, a low-lying area southwest of Suminoe Park, was filled in by about 360,000 volunteers over two years. The shrine was designated a Gokoku Shrine by the Interior Minister and was enshrined on May 4, 1940 (Shōwa 15), initially in temporary buildings due to shortages of materials and labor caused by the war.

In 1960 (Shōwa 35), a new shrine building was constructed, and the shrine was officially moved to its current location in 1963 (Shōwa 38). The shrine was visited by Emperor Shōwa and Empress Kōjun on July 15, 1970, and by the Crown Prince and Princess (now Emperor Emeritus Akihito and Empress Emerita Michiko) on May 31, 1978.

'''Osaka Gokoku Shrine''' (おおさかごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Osaka]], Japan. It enshrines over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture, making it the third largest Gokoku Shrine in terms of the number of enshrined deities, following [[Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]] and [[Fukuoka Gokoku Shrine]]. The shrine is a [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
The shrine is located southwest of [[Suminoe Park]] and adjacent to the [[Suminoe Boat Racecourse]]. The main torii gate, which faces [[Suminoe Avenue]], is the largest in Osaka Prefecture.
== History == The first attempt to enshrine Osaka’s war dead was made on April 10, 1871 (Meiji 4), with the creation of a [[Shōkonsha]] within the [[Sanadayama Army Cemetery]] grounds. However, after the [[Satsuma Rebellion]], memorial services were held at various locations, including [[Osaka Castle]] and the monument to Emperor Meiji at [[Nakanoshima]], before finally moving to [[Jōtō Drill Ground]] in 1900 (Meiji 33). The original Shōkonsha at Sanadayama was abolished by 1918 (Taishō 7).
Due to the growing reverence for war dead following the outbreak of the [[Second Sino-Japanese War]], the Governor of Osaka established the Gokoku Shrine Construction Support Association in 1938 (Shōwa 13). The site, a low-lying area southwest of Suminoe Park, was filled in by about 360,000 volunteers over two years. The shrine was designated a Gokoku Shrine by the Interior Minister and was enshrined on May 4, 1940 (Shōwa 15), initially in temporary buildings due to shortages of materials and labor caused by the war.
After World War II, the shrine was renamed "Naniwa-no-Miya" and enshrined Emperor Nintoku in response to the GHQ’s Shinto Directive. The shrine was restored to its original name, Osaka Gokoku Shrine, following the signing of the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952 (Shōwa 27).
In 1960 (Shōwa 35), a new shrine building was constructed, and the shrine was officially moved to its current location in 1963 (Shōwa 38). The shrine was visited by Emperor Shōwa and Empress Kōjun on July 15, 1970, and by the Crown Prince and Princess (now Emperor Emeritus Akihito and Empress Emerita Michiko) on May 31, 1978.
A statue honoring special attack forces was erected on October 24, 2009 (Heisei 21).
== Deities == * Main deity - Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture (exactly 105,665)
== Clergy and Activities == * Former chief priest Chūmaro Yanagisawa served as the Osaka executive chairman of [[Nippon Kaigi]] and the Osaka prefectural head of the [[Shinto Seiji Renmei]]. He passed away on March 31, 2018. * Masachika Fujie became chief priest in 2021 (Reiwa 3). He organized a memorial service for former Prime Minister [[Shinzo Abe]] following his assassination.
== Grounds == * main hall - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Heiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Haiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Hōanden - Enshrines [[Emperor Nintoku]] and Admiral [[Tōgō Heihachirō]] * Sorei-sha "Homare-no-Miya" * festival Warehouses * Administrative Offices * Suminoe Hall * Ritual Hall (Former Gokoku Dojo) * Garden * Memorials for various military units
== Access == * Near [[Suminoe Koen Station]] on the [[Osaka Metro Yotsubashi Line]] and [[Osaka Metro Nanko Port Town Line|New Tram]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == {{commonscat|Osaka-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Suminoe Park]]
== External Links == * [https://www.osakagokoku.or.jp/ Osaka Gokoku Shrine Official Website]

'''Osaka Gokoku Shrine''' (おおさかごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Osaka]], Japan. It enshrines over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture, making it the third largest Gokoku Shrine in terms of the number of enshrined deities, following [[Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]] and [[Fukuoka Gokoku Shrine]]. The shrine is a [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
The shrine is located southwest of [[Suminoe Park]] and adjacent to the [[Suminoe Boat Racecourse]]. The main torii gate, which faces [[Suminoe Avenue]], is the largest in Osaka Prefecture.
== History == The first attempt to enshrine Osaka’s war dead was made on April 10, 1871 (Meiji 4), with the creation of a [[Shōkonsha]] within the [[Sanadayama Army Cemetery]] grounds. However, after the [[Satsuma Rebellion]], memorial services were held at various locations, including [[Osaka Castle]] and the monument to Emperor Meiji at [[Nakanoshima]], before finally moving to [[Jōtō Drill Ground]] in 1900 (Meiji 33). The original Shōkonsha at Sanadayama was abolished by 1918 (Taishō 7).
Due to the growing reverence for war dead following the outbreak of the [[Second Sino-Japanese War]], the Governor of Osaka established the Gokoku Shrine Construction Support Association in 1938 (Shōwa 13). The site, a low-lying area southwest of Suminoe Park, was filled in by about 360,000 volunteers over two years. The shrine was designated a Gokoku Shrine by the Interior Minister and was enshrined on May 4, 1940 (Shōwa 15), initially in temporary buildings due to shortages of materials and labor caused by the war.
After World War II, the shrine was renamed "Naniwa-no-Miya" and enshrined Emperor Nintoku in response to the GHQ’s Shinto Directive. The shrine was restored to its original name, Osaka Gokoku Shrine, following the signing of the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952 (Shōwa 27).
In 1960 (Shōwa 35), a new shrine building was constructed, and the shrine was officially moved to its current location in 1963 (Shōwa 38). The shrine was visited by Emperor Shōwa and Empress Kōjun on July 15, 1970, and by the Crown Prince and Princess (now Emperor Emeritus Akihito and Empress Emerita Michiko) on May 31, 1978.
A statue honoring special attack forces was erected on October 24, 2009 (Heisei 21).
== Deities == * Main deity - Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture (exactly 105,665)
== Clergy and Activities == * Former chief priest Chūmaro Yanagisawa served as the Osaka executive chairman of [[Nippon Kaigi]] and the Osaka prefectural head of the [[Shinto Seiji Renmei]]. He passed away on March 31, 2018. * Masachika Fujie became chief priest in 2021 (Reiwa 3). He organized a memorial service for former Prime Minister [[Shinzo Abe]] following his assassination.
== Grounds == * main hall - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Heiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Haiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Hōanden - Enshrines [[Emperor Nintoku]] and Admiral [[Tōgō Heihachirō]] * Sorei-sha "Homare-no-Miya" * festival Warehouses * Administrative Offices * Suminoe Hall * Ritual Hall (Former Gokoku Dojo) * Garden * Memorials for various military units
== Access == * Near [[Suminoe Koen Station]] on the [[Osaka Metro Yotsubashi Line]] and [[Osaka Metro Nanko Port Town Line|New Tram]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == {{commonscat|Osaka-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Suminoe Park]]
== External Links == * [https://www.osakagokoku.or.jp/ Osaka Gokoku Shrine Official Website]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Osaka Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Osaka-gokoku-jinja1.jpg|250px|Osaka Gokoku Shrine]] | location = [[Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]], [[Osaka City|Osaka]], [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Minamikagaya]] 1-1-77 | deity = Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture | type = Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Shrine, [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = 1940 (Showa 15) | annual_festival = [[May 20|May 20 (Spring)]] ・ [[October 20|October 20 (Autumn)]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|36|36.5|N|135|28|26.1|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Osaka Gokoku Shrine''' (おおさかごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Osaka]], Japan. It enshrines over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture, making it the third largest Gokoku Shrine in terms of the number of enshrined deities, following [[Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]] and [[Fukuoka Gokoku Shrine]]. The shrine is a [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
The shrine is located southwest of [[Suminoe Park]] and adjacent to the [[Suminoe Boat Racecourse]]. The main torii gate, which faces [[Suminoe Avenue]], is the largest in Osaka Prefecture.
== History == The first attempt to enshrine Osaka’s war dead was made on April 10, 1871 (Meiji 4), with the creation of a [[Shōkonsha]] within the [[Sanadayama Army Cemetery]] grounds. However, after the [[Satsuma Rebellion]], memorial services were held at various locations, including [[Osaka Castle]] and the monument to Emperor Meiji at [[Nakanoshima]], before finally moving to [[Jōtō Drill Ground]] in 1900 (Meiji 33). The original Shōkonsha at Sanadayama was abolished by 1918 (Taishō 7).
Due to the growing reverence for war dead following the outbreak of the [[Second Sino-Japanese War]], the Governor of Osaka established the Gokoku Shrine Construction Support Association in 1938 (Shōwa 13). The site, a low-lying area southwest of Suminoe Park, was filled in by about 360,000 volunteers over two years. The shrine was designated a Gokoku Shrine by the Interior Minister and was enshrined on May 4, 1940 (Shōwa 15), initially in temporary buildings due to shortages of materials and labor caused by the war.
After World War II, the shrine was renamed "Naniwa-no-Miya" and enshrined Emperor Nintoku in response to the GHQ’s Shinto Directive. The shrine was restored to its original name, Osaka Gokoku Shrine, following the signing of the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952 (Shōwa 27).
In 1960 (Shōwa 35), a new shrine building was constructed, and the shrine was officially moved to its current location in 1963 (Shōwa 38). The shrine was visited by Emperor Shōwa and Empress Kōjun on July 15, 1970, and by the Crown Prince and Princess (now Emperor Emeritus Akihito and Empress Emerita Michiko) on May 31, 1978.
A statue honoring special attack forces was erected on October 24, 2009 (Heisei 21).
== Deities == * Main deity - Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture (exactly 105,665)
== Clergy and Activities == * Former chief priest Chūmaro Yanagisawa served as the Osaka executive chairman of [[Nippon Kaigi]] and the Osaka prefectural head of the [[Shinto Seiji Renmei]]. He passed away on March 31, 2018. * Masachika Fujie became chief priest in 2021 (Reiwa 3). He organized a memorial service for former Prime Minister [[Shinzo Abe]] following his assassination.
== Grounds == * main hall - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Heiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Haiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Hōanden - Enshrines [[Emperor Nintoku]] and Admiral [[Tōgō Heihachirō]] * Sorei-sha "Homare-no-Miya" * festival Warehouses * Administrative Offices * Suminoe Hall * Ritual Hall (Former Gokoku Dojo) * Garden * Memorials for various military units
== Access == * Near [[Suminoe Koen Station]] on the [[Osaka Metro Yotsubashi Line]] and [[Osaka Metro Nanko Port Town Line|New Tram]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == {{commonscat|Osaka-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Suminoe Park]]
== External Links == * [https://www.osakagokoku.or.jp/ Osaka Gokoku Shrine Official Website]

It enshrines over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture, making it the third largest Gokoku Shrine in terms of the number of enshrined deities, following [[Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]] and [[Fukuoka Gokoku Shrine]]. The shrine is a [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
The shrine is located southwest of [[Suminoe Park]] and adjacent to the [[Suminoe Boat Racecourse]]. The main torii gate, which faces [[Suminoe Avenue]], is the largest in Osaka Prefecture.
== History == The first attempt to enshrine Osaka’s war dead was made on April 10, 1871 (Meiji 4), with the creation of a [[Shōkonsha]] within the [[Sanadayama Army Cemetery]] grounds. A statue honoring special attack forces was erected on October 24, 2009 (Heisei 21).
== Deities == * Main deity - Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture (exactly 105,665)
== Clergy and Activities == * Former chief priest Chūmaro Yanagisawa served as the Osaka executive chairman of [[Nippon Kaigi]] and the Osaka prefectural head of the [[Shinto Seiji Renmei]]. He passed away on March 31, 2018. * Masachika Fujie became chief priest in 2021 (Reiwa 3). He organized a memorial service for former Prime Minister [[Shinzo Abe]] following his assassination.
== Grounds == * main hall - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Heiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Haiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Hōanden - Enshrines [[Emperor Nintoku]] and Admiral [[Tōgō Heihachirō]] * Sorei-sha "Homare-no-Miya" * festival Warehouses * Administrative Offices * Suminoe Hall * Ritual Hall (Former Gokoku Dojo) * Garden * Memorials for various military units

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Osaka Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Osaka-gokoku-jinja1.jpg|250px|Osaka Gokoku Shrine]] | location = [[Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]], [[Osaka City|Osaka]], [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Minamikagaya]] 1-1-77 | deity = Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture | type = Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Shrine, [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = 1940 (Showa 15) | annual_festival = [[May 20|May 20 (Spring)]] ・ [[October 20|October 20 (Autumn)]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|36|36.5|N|135|28|26.1|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Osaka Gokoku Shrine''' (おおさかごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Osaka]], Japan. It enshrines over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture, making it the third largest Gokoku Shrine in terms of the number of enshrined deities, following [[Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]] and [[Fukuoka Gokoku Shrine]]. The shrine is a [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
The shrine is located southwest of [[Suminoe Park]] and adjacent to the [[Suminoe Boat Racecourse]]. The main torii gate, which faces [[Suminoe Avenue]], is the largest in Osaka Prefecture.
== History == The first attempt to enshrine Osaka’s war dead was made on April 10, 1871 (Meiji 4), with the creation of a [[Shōkonsha]] within the [[Sanadayama Army Cemetery]] grounds. However, after the [[Satsuma Rebellion]], memorial services were held at various locations, including [[Osaka Castle]] and the monument to Emperor Meiji at [[Nakanoshima]], before finally moving to [[Jōtō Drill Ground]] in 1900 (Meiji 33). The original Shōkonsha at Sanadayama was abolished by 1918 (Taishō 7).
Due to the growing reverence for war dead following the outbreak of the [[Second Sino-Japanese War]], the Governor of Osaka established the Gokoku Shrine Construction Support Association in 1938 (Shōwa 13). The site, a low-lying area southwest of Suminoe Park, was filled in by about 360,000 volunteers over two years. The shrine was designated a Gokoku Shrine by the Interior Minister and was enshrined on May 4, 1940 (Shōwa 15), initially in temporary buildings due to shortages of materials and labor caused by the war.
After World War II, the shrine was renamed "Naniwa-no-Miya" and enshrined Emperor Nintoku in response to the GHQ’s Shinto Directive. The shrine was restored to its original name, Osaka Gokoku Shrine, following the signing of the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952 (Shōwa 27).
In 1960 (Shōwa 35), a new shrine building was constructed, and the shrine was officially moved to its current location in 1963 (Shōwa 38). The shrine was visited by Emperor Shōwa and Empress Kōjun on July 15, 1970, and by the Crown Prince and Princess (now Emperor Emeritus Akihito and Empress Emerita Michiko) on May 31, 1978.
A statue honoring special attack forces was erected on October 24, 2009 (Heisei 21).
== Deities == * Main deity - Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture (exactly 105,665)
== Clergy and Activities == * Former chief priest Chūmaro Yanagisawa served as the Osaka executive chairman of [[Nippon Kaigi]] and the Osaka prefectural head of the [[Shinto Seiji Renmei]]. He passed away on March 31, 2018. * Masachika Fujie became chief priest in 2021 (Reiwa 3). He organized a memorial service for former Prime Minister [[Shinzo Abe]] following his assassination.
== Grounds == * main hall - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Heiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Haiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Hōanden - Enshrines [[Emperor Nintoku]] and Admiral [[Tōgō Heihachirō]] * Sorei-sha "Homare-no-Miya" * festival Warehouses * Administrative Offices * Suminoe Hall * Ritual Hall (Former Gokoku Dojo) * Garden * Memorials for various military units
== Access == * Near [[Suminoe Koen Station]] on the [[Osaka Metro Yotsubashi Line]] and [[Osaka Metro Nanko Port Town Line|New Tram]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == {{commonscat|Osaka-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Suminoe Park]]
== External Links == * [https://www.osakagokoku.or.jp/ Osaka Gokoku Shrine Official Website]

The shrine is a [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Osaka Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Osaka-gokoku-jinja1.jpg|250px|Osaka Gokoku Shrine]] | location = [[Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]], [[Osaka City|Osaka]], [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Minamikagaya]] 1-1-77 | deity = Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture | type = Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Shrine, [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = 1940 (Showa 15) | annual_festival = [[May 20|May 20 (Spring)]] ・ [[October 20|October 20 (Autumn)]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|36|36.5|N|135|28|26.1|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Osaka Gokoku Shrine''' (おおさかごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Osaka]], Japan. It enshrines over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture, making it the third largest Gokoku Shrine in terms of the number of enshrined deities, following [[Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]] and [[Fukuoka Gokoku Shrine]]. The shrine is a [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
The shrine is located southwest of [[Suminoe Park]] and adjacent to the [[Suminoe Boat Racecourse]]. The main torii gate, which faces [[Suminoe Avenue]], is the largest in Osaka Prefecture.
== History == The first attempt to enshrine Osaka’s war dead was made on April 10, 1871 (Meiji 4), with the creation of a [[Shōkonsha]] within the [[Sanadayama Army Cemetery]] grounds. However, after the [[Satsuma Rebellion]], memorial services were held at various locations, including [[Osaka Castle]] and the monument to Emperor Meiji at [[Nakanoshima]], before finally moving to [[Jōtō Drill Ground]] in 1900 (Meiji 33). The original Shōkonsha at Sanadayama was abolished by 1918 (Taishō 7).
Due to the growing reverence for war dead following the outbreak of the [[Second Sino-Japanese War]], the Governor of Osaka established the Gokoku Shrine Construction Support Association in 1938 (Shōwa 13). The site, a low-lying area southwest of Suminoe Park, was filled in by about 360,000 volunteers over two years. The shrine was designated a Gokoku Shrine by the Interior Minister and was enshrined on May 4, 1940 (Shōwa 15), initially in temporary buildings due to shortages of materials and labor caused by the war.
After World War II, the shrine was renamed "Naniwa-no-Miya" and enshrined Emperor Nintoku in response to the GHQ’s Shinto Directive. The shrine was restored to its original name, Osaka Gokoku Shrine, following the signing of the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952 (Shōwa 27).
In 1960 (Shōwa 35), a new shrine building was constructed, and the shrine was officially moved to its current location in 1963 (Shōwa 38). The shrine was visited by Emperor Shōwa and Empress Kōjun on July 15, 1970, and by the Crown Prince and Princess (now Emperor Emeritus Akihito and Empress Emerita Michiko) on May 31, 1978.
A statue honoring special attack forces was erected on October 24, 2009 (Heisei 21).
== Deities == * Main deity - Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture (exactly 105,665)
== Clergy and Activities == * Former chief priest Chūmaro Yanagisawa served as the Osaka executive chairman of [[Nippon Kaigi]] and the Osaka prefectural head of the [[Shinto Seiji Renmei]]. He passed away on March 31, 2018. * Masachika Fujie became chief priest in 2021 (Reiwa 3). He organized a memorial service for former Prime Minister [[Shinzo Abe]] following his assassination.
== Grounds == * main hall - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Heiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Haiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Hōanden - Enshrines [[Emperor Nintoku]] and Admiral [[Tōgō Heihachirō]] * Sorei-sha "Homare-no-Miya" * festival Warehouses * Administrative Offices * Suminoe Hall * Ritual Hall (Former Gokoku Dojo) * Garden * Memorials for various military units
== Access == * Near [[Suminoe Koen Station]] on the [[Osaka Metro Yotsubashi Line]] and [[Osaka Metro Nanko Port Town Line|New Tram]]

== See Also == {{commonscat|Osaka-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Suminoe Park]]

'''Osaka Gokoku Shrine''' (おおさかごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Osaka]], Japan. It enshrines over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture, making it the third largest Gokoku Shrine in terms of the number of enshrined deities, following [[Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]] and [[Fukuoka Gokoku Shrine]]. The shrine is a [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
The shrine is located southwest of [[Suminoe Park]] and adjacent to the [[Suminoe Boat Racecourse]]. The main torii gate, which faces [[Suminoe Avenue]], is the largest in Osaka Prefecture.
== History == The first attempt to enshrine Osaka’s war dead was made on April 10, 1871 (Meiji 4), with the creation of a [[Shōkonsha]] within the [[Sanadayama Army Cemetery]] grounds. However, after the [[Satsuma Rebellion]], memorial services were held at various locations, including [[Osaka Castle]] and the monument to Emperor Meiji at [[Nakanoshima]], before finally moving to [[Jōtō Drill Ground]] in 1900 (Meiji 33). The original Shōkonsha at Sanadayama was abolished by 1918 (Taishō 7).
Due to the growing reverence for war dead following the outbreak of the [[Second Sino-Japanese War]], the Governor of Osaka established the Gokoku Shrine Construction Support Association in 1938 (Shōwa 13). The site, a low-lying area southwest of Suminoe Park, was filled in by about 360,000 volunteers over two years. The shrine was designated a Gokoku Shrine by the Interior Minister and was enshrined on May 4, 1940 (Shōwa 15), initially in temporary buildings due to shortages of materials and labor caused by the war.
After World War II, the shrine was renamed "Naniwa-no-Miya" and enshrined Emperor Nintoku in response to the GHQ’s Shinto Directive. The shrine was restored to its original name, Osaka Gokoku Shrine, following the signing of the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952 (Shōwa 27).
In 1960 (Shōwa 35), a new shrine building was constructed, and the shrine was officially moved to its current location in 1963 (Shōwa 38). The shrine was visited by Emperor Shōwa and Empress Kōjun on July 15, 1970, and by the Crown Prince and Princess (now Emperor Emeritus Akihito and Empress Emerita Michiko) on May 31, 1978.
A statue honoring special attack forces was erected on October 24, 2009 (Heisei 21).
== Deities == * Main deity - Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture (exactly 105,665)
== Clergy and Activities == * Former chief priest Chūmaro Yanagisawa served as the Osaka executive chairman of [[Nippon Kaigi]] and the Osaka prefectural head of the [[Shinto Seiji Renmei]]. The original Shōkonsha at Sanadayama was abolished by 1918 (Taishō 7).
Due to the growing reverence for war dead following the outbreak of the [[Second Sino-Japanese War]], the Governor of Osaka established the Gokoku Shrine Construction Support Association in 1938 (Shōwa 13). The site, a low-lying area southwest of Suminoe Park, was filled in by about 360,000 volunteers over two years. The shrine was designated a Gokoku Shrine by the Interior Minister and was enshrined on May 4, 1940 (Shōwa 15), initially in temporary buildings due to shortages of materials and labor caused by the war.
After World War II, the shrine was renamed "Naniwa-no-Miya" and enshrined Emperor Nintoku in response to the GHQ’s Shinto Directive. The shrine was restored to its original name, Osaka Gokoku Shrine, following the signing of the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952 (Shōwa 27).
In 1960 (Shōwa 35), a new shrine building was constructed, and the shrine was officially moved to its current location in 1963 (Shōwa 38). The shrine was visited by Emperor Shōwa and Empress Kōjun on July 15, 1970, and by the Crown Prince and Princess (now Emperor Emeritus Akihito and Empress Emerita Michiko) on May 31, 1978.
A statue honoring special attack forces was erected on October 24, 2009 (Heisei 21).
== Deities == * Main deity - Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture (exactly 105,665)
== Clergy and Activities == * Former chief priest Chūmaro Yanagisawa served as the Osaka executive chairman of [[Nippon Kaigi]] and the Osaka prefectural head of the [[Shinto Seiji Renmei]]. He passed away on March 31, 2018. * Masachika Fujie became chief priest in 2021 (Reiwa 3). He organized a memorial service for former Prime Minister [[Shinzo Abe]] following his assassination.
== Grounds == * main hall - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Heiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Haiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Hōanden - Enshrines [[Emperor Nintoku]] and Admiral [[Tōgō Heihachirō]] * Sorei-sha "Homare-no-Miya" * festival Warehouses * Administrative Offices * Suminoe Hall * Ritual Hall (Former Gokoku Dojo) * Garden * Memorials for various military units

'''Osaka Gokoku Shrine''' (おおさかごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Osaka]], Japan. It enshrines over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture, making it the third largest Gokoku Shrine in terms of the number of enshrined deities, following [[Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]] and [[Fukuoka Gokoku Shrine]]. The shrine is a [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
The shrine is located southwest of [[Suminoe Park]] and adjacent to the [[Suminoe Boat Racecourse]]. The main torii gate, which faces [[Suminoe Avenue]], is the largest in Osaka Prefecture.

The shrine was visited by Emperor Shōwa and Empress Kōjun on July 15, 1970, and by the Crown Prince and Princess (now Emperor Emeritus Akihito and Empress Emerita Michiko) on May 31, 1978.

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Osaka Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Osaka-gokoku-jinja1.jpg|250px|Osaka Gokoku Shrine]] | location = [[Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]], [[Osaka City|Osaka]], [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Minamikagaya]] 1-1-77 | deity = Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture | type = Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Shrine, [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = 1940 (Showa 15) | annual_festival = [[May 20|May 20 (Spring)]] ・ [[October 20|October 20 (Autumn)]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|36|36.5|N|135|28|26.1|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Osaka Gokoku Shrine''' (おおさかごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Osaka]], Japan. It enshrines over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture, making it the third largest Gokoku Shrine in terms of the number of enshrined deities, following [[Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]] and [[Fukuoka Gokoku Shrine]]. The shrine is a [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
The shrine is located southwest of [[Suminoe Park]] and adjacent to the [[Suminoe Boat Racecourse]]. The main torii gate, which faces [[Suminoe Avenue]], is the largest in Osaka Prefecture.
== History == The first attempt to enshrine Osaka’s war dead was made on April 10, 1871 (Meiji 4), with the creation of a [[Shōkonsha]] within the [[Sanadayama Army Cemetery]] grounds. However, after the [[Satsuma Rebellion]], memorial services were held at various locations, including [[Osaka Castle]] and the monument to Emperor Meiji at [[Nakanoshima]], before finally moving to [[Jōtō Drill Ground]] in 1900 (Meiji 33). The original Shōkonsha at Sanadayama was abolished by 1918 (Taishō 7).
Due to the growing reverence for war dead following the outbreak of the [[Second Sino-Japanese War]], the Governor of Osaka established the Gokoku Shrine Construction Support Association in 1938 (Shōwa 13). The site, a low-lying area southwest of Suminoe Park, was filled in by about 360,000 volunteers over two years. The shrine was designated a Gokoku Shrine by the Interior Minister and was enshrined on May 4, 1940 (Shōwa 15), initially in temporary buildings due to shortages of materials and labor caused by the war.
After World War II, the shrine was renamed "Naniwa-no-Miya" and enshrined Emperor Nintoku in response to the GHQ’s Shinto Directive. The shrine was restored to its original name, Osaka Gokoku Shrine, following the signing of the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952 (Shōwa 27).
In 1960 (Shōwa 35), a new shrine building was constructed, and the shrine was officially moved to its current location in 1963 (Shōwa 38). The shrine was visited by Emperor Shōwa and Empress Kōjun on July 15, 1970, and by the Crown Prince and Princess (now Emperor Emeritus Akihito and Empress Emerita Michiko) on May 31, 1978.
A statue honoring special attack forces was erected on October 24, 2009 (Heisei 21).
== Deities == * Main deity - Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture (exactly 105,665)
== Clergy and Activities == * Former chief priest Chūmaro Yanagisawa served as the Osaka executive chairman of [[Nippon Kaigi]] and the Osaka prefectural head of the [[Shinto Seiji Renmei]]. He passed away on March 31, 2018. * Masachika Fujie became chief priest in 2021 (Reiwa 3). He organized a memorial service for former Prime Minister [[Shinzo Abe]] following his assassination.
== Grounds == * main hall - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Heiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Haiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Hōanden - Enshrines [[Emperor Nintoku]] and Admiral [[Tōgō Heihachirō]] * Sorei-sha "Homare-no-Miya" * festival Warehouses * Administrative Offices * Suminoe Hall * Ritual Hall (Former Gokoku Dojo) * Garden * Memorials for various military units

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Osaka Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Osaka-gokoku-jinja1.jpg|250px|Osaka Gokoku Shrine]] | location = [[Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]], [[Osaka City|Osaka]], [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Minamikagaya]] 1-1-77 | deity = Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture | type = Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Shrine, [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = 1940 (Showa 15) | annual_festival = [[May 20|May 20 (Spring)]] ・ [[October 20|October 20 (Autumn)]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|36|36.5|N|135|28|26.1|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Osaka Gokoku Shrine''' (おおさかごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Osaka]], Japan. It enshrines over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture, making it the third largest Gokoku Shrine in terms of the number of enshrined deities, following [[Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]] and [[Fukuoka Gokoku Shrine]]. The shrine is a [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
The shrine is located southwest of [[Suminoe Park]] and adjacent to the [[Suminoe Boat Racecourse]]. The main torii gate, which faces [[Suminoe Avenue]], is the largest in Osaka Prefecture.
== History == The first attempt to enshrine Osaka’s war dead was made on April 10, 1871 (Meiji 4), with the creation of a [[Shōkonsha]] within the [[Sanadayama Army Cemetery]] grounds. However, after the [[Satsuma Rebellion]], memorial services were held at various locations, including [[Osaka Castle]] and the monument to Emperor Meiji at [[Nakanoshima]], before finally moving to [[Jōtō Drill Ground]] in 1900 (Meiji 33). The original Shōkonsha at Sanadayama was abolished by 1918 (Taishō 7).
Due to the growing reverence for war dead following the outbreak of the [[Second Sino-Japanese War]], the Governor of Osaka established the Gokoku Shrine Construction Support Association in 1938 (Shōwa 13). The site, a low-lying area southwest of Suminoe Park, was filled in by about 360,000 volunteers over two years. The shrine was designated a Gokoku Shrine by the Interior Minister and was enshrined on May 4, 1940 (Shōwa 15), initially in temporary buildings due to shortages of materials and labor caused by the war.
After World War II, the shrine was renamed "Naniwa-no-Miya" and enshrined Emperor Nintoku in response to the GHQ’s Shinto Directive. The shrine was restored to its original name, Osaka Gokoku Shrine, following the signing of the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952 (Shōwa 27).
In 1960 (Shōwa 35), a new shrine building was constructed, and the shrine was officially moved to its current location in 1963 (Shōwa 38). The shrine was visited by Emperor Shōwa and Empress Kōjun on July 15, 1970, and by the Crown Prince and Princess (now Emperor Emeritus Akihito and Empress Emerita Michiko) on May 31, 1978.
A statue honoring special attack forces was erected on October 24, 2009 (Heisei 21).
== Deities == * Main deity - Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture (exactly 105,665)
== Clergy and Activities == * Former chief priest Chūmaro Yanagisawa served as the Osaka executive chairman of [[Nippon Kaigi]] and the Osaka prefectural head of the [[Shinto Seiji Renmei]]. He passed away on March 31, 2018. * Masachika Fujie became chief priest in 2021 (Reiwa 3). He organized a memorial service for former Prime Minister [[Shinzo Abe]] following his assassination.
== Grounds == * main hall - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Heiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Haiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Hōanden - Enshrines [[Emperor Nintoku]] and Admiral [[Tōgō Heihachirō]] * Sorei-sha "Homare-no-Miya" * festival Warehouses * Administrative Offices * Suminoe Hall * Ritual Hall (Former Gokoku Dojo) * Garden * Memorials for various military units
== Access == * Near [[Suminoe Koen Station]] on the [[Osaka Metro Yotsubashi Line]] and [[Osaka Metro Nanko Port Town Line|New Tram]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Osaka Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Osaka-gokoku-jinja1.jpg|250px|Osaka Gokoku Shrine]] | location = [[Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]], [[Osaka City|Osaka]], [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Minamikagaya]] 1-1-77 | deity = Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture | type = Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Shrine, [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = 1940 (Showa 15) | annual_festival = [[May 20|May 20 (Spring)]] ・ [[October 20|October 20 (Autumn)]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|36|36.5|N|135|28|26.1|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Osaka Gokoku Shrine''' (おおさかごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Osaka]], Japan. It enshrines over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture, making it the third largest Gokoku Shrine in terms of the number of enshrined deities, following [[Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]] and [[Fukuoka Gokoku Shrine]]. The shrine is a [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
The shrine is located southwest of [[Suminoe Park]] and adjacent to the [[Suminoe Boat Racecourse]]. The main torii gate, which faces [[Suminoe Avenue]], is the largest in Osaka Prefecture.
== History == The first attempt to enshrine Osaka’s war dead was made on April 10, 1871 (Meiji 4), with the creation of a [[Shōkonsha]] within the [[Sanadayama Army Cemetery]] grounds. However, after the [[Satsuma Rebellion]], memorial services were held at various locations, including [[Osaka Castle]] and the monument to Emperor Meiji at [[Nakanoshima]], before finally moving to [[Jōtō Drill Ground]] in 1900 (Meiji 33). The original Shōkonsha at Sanadayama was abolished by 1918 (Taishō 7).
Due to the growing reverence for war dead following the outbreak of the [[Second Sino-Japanese War]], the Governor of Osaka established the Gokoku Shrine Construction Support Association in 1938 (Shōwa 13). The site, a low-lying area southwest of Suminoe Park, was filled in by about 360,000 volunteers over two years. The shrine was designated a Gokoku Shrine by the Interior Minister and was enshrined on May 4, 1940 (Shōwa 15), initially in temporary buildings due to shortages of materials and labor caused by the war.
After World War II, the shrine was renamed "Naniwa-no-Miya" and enshrined Emperor Nintoku in response to the GHQ’s Shinto Directive. The shrine was restored to its original name, Osaka Gokoku Shrine, following the signing of the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952 (Shōwa 27).
In 1960 (Shōwa 35), a new shrine building was constructed, and the shrine was officially moved to its current location in 1963 (Shōwa 38). The shrine was visited by Emperor Shōwa and Empress Kōjun on July 15, 1970, and by the Crown Prince and Princess (now Emperor Emeritus Akihito and Empress Emerita Michiko) on May 31, 1978.
A statue honoring special attack forces was erected on October 24, 2009 (Heisei 21).
== Deities == * Main deity - Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture (exactly 105,665)
== Clergy and Activities == * Former chief priest Chūmaro Yanagisawa served as the Osaka executive chairman of [[Nippon Kaigi]] and the Osaka prefectural head of the [[Shinto Seiji Renmei]]. He passed away on March 31, 2018. * Masachika Fujie became chief priest in 2021 (Reiwa 3). He organized a memorial service for former Prime Minister [[Shinzo Abe]] following his assassination.
== Grounds == * main hall - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Heiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Haiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Hōanden - Enshrines [[Emperor Nintoku]] and Admiral [[Tōgō Heihachirō]] * Sorei-sha "Homare-no-Miya" * festival Warehouses * Administrative Offices * Suminoe Hall * Ritual Hall (Former Gokoku Dojo) * Garden * Memorials for various military units
== Access == * Near [[Suminoe Koen Station]] on the [[Osaka Metro Yotsubashi Line]] and [[Osaka Metro Nanko Port Town Line|New Tram]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Osaka Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Osaka-gokoku-jinja1.jpg|250px|Osaka Gokoku Shrine]] | location = [[Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]], [[Osaka City|Osaka]], [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Minamikagaya]] 1-1-77 | deity = Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture | type = Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Shrine, [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = 1940 (Showa 15) | annual_festival = [[May 20|May 20 (Spring)]] ・ [[October 20|October 20 (Autumn)]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|36|36.5|N|135|28|26.1|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Osaka Gokoku Shrine''' (おおさかごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Osaka]], Japan. It enshrines over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture, making it the third largest Gokoku Shrine in terms of the number of enshrined deities, following [[Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]] and [[Fukuoka Gokoku Shrine]]. The shrine is a [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
The shrine is located southwest of [[Suminoe Park]] and adjacent to the [[Suminoe Boat Racecourse]]. The main torii gate, which faces [[Suminoe Avenue]], is the largest in Osaka Prefecture.
== History == The first attempt to enshrine Osaka’s war dead was made on April 10, 1871 (Meiji 4), with the creation of a [[Shōkonsha]] within the [[Sanadayama Army Cemetery]] grounds. However, after the [[Satsuma Rebellion]], memorial services were held at various locations, including [[Osaka Castle]] and the monument to Emperor Meiji at [[Nakanoshima]], before finally moving to [[Jōtō Drill Ground]] in 1900 (Meiji 33). The original Shōkonsha at Sanadayama was abolished by 1918 (Taishō 7).
Due to the growing reverence for war dead following the outbreak of the [[Second Sino-Japanese War]], the Governor of Osaka established the Gokoku Shrine Construction Support Association in 1938 (Shōwa 13). The site, a low-lying area southwest of Suminoe Park, was filled in by about 360,000 volunteers over two years. The shrine was designated a Gokoku Shrine by the Interior Minister and was enshrined on May 4, 1940 (Shōwa 15), initially in temporary buildings due to shortages of materials and labor caused by the war.
After World War II, the shrine was renamed "Naniwa-no-Miya" and enshrined Emperor Nintoku in response to the GHQ’s Shinto Directive. The shrine was restored to its original name, Osaka Gokoku Shrine, following the signing of the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952 (Shōwa 27).
In 1960 (Shōwa 35), a new shrine building was constructed, and the shrine was officially moved to its current location in 1963 (Shōwa 38). The shrine was visited by Emperor Shōwa and Empress Kōjun on July 15, 1970, and by the Crown Prince and Princess (now Emperor Emeritus Akihito and Empress Emerita Michiko) on May 31, 1978.
A statue honoring special attack forces was erected on October 24, 2009 (Heisei 21).
== Deities == * Main deity - Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture (exactly 105,665)
== Clergy and Activities == * Former chief priest Chūmaro Yanagisawa served as the Osaka executive chairman of [[Nippon Kaigi]] and the Osaka prefectural head of the [[Shinto Seiji Renmei]]. He passed away on March 31, 2018. * Masachika Fujie became chief priest in 2021 (Reiwa 3). He organized a memorial service for former Prime Minister [[Shinzo Abe]] following his assassination.
== Grounds == * main hall - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Heiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Haiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Hōanden - Enshrines [[Emperor Nintoku]] and Admiral [[Tōgō Heihachirō]] * Sorei-sha "Homare-no-Miya" * festival Warehouses * Administrative Offices * Suminoe Hall * Ritual Hall (Former Gokoku Dojo) * Garden * Memorials for various military units

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Osaka Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Osaka-gokoku-jinja1.jpg|250px|Osaka Gokoku Shrine]] | location = [[Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]], [[Osaka City|Osaka]], [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Minamikagaya]] 1-1-77 | deity = Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture | type = Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Shrine, [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | founded = 1940 (Showa 15) | annual_festival = [[May 20|May 20 (Spring)]] ・ [[October 20|October 20 (Autumn)]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|36|36.5|N|135|28|26.1|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Osaka Gokoku Shrine''' (おおさかごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Suminoe-ku, Osaka|Suminoe-ku]], [[Osaka]], Japan. It enshrines over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture, making it the third largest Gokoku Shrine in terms of the number of enshrined deities, following [[Okinawa Gokoku Shrine]] and [[Fukuoka Gokoku Shrine]]. The shrine is a [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
The shrine is located southwest of [[Suminoe Park]] and adjacent to the [[Suminoe Boat Racecourse]]. The main torii gate, which faces [[Suminoe Avenue]], is the largest in Osaka Prefecture.
== History == The first attempt to enshrine Osaka’s war dead was made on April 10, 1871 (Meiji 4), with the creation of a [[Shōkonsha]] within the [[Sanadayama Army Cemetery]] grounds. However, after the [[Satsuma Rebellion]], memorial services were held at various locations, including [[Osaka Castle]] and the monument to Emperor Meiji at [[Nakanoshima]], before finally moving to [[Jōtō Drill Ground]] in 1900 (Meiji 33). The original Shōkonsha at Sanadayama was abolished by 1918 (Taishō 7).
Due to the growing reverence for war dead following the outbreak of the [[Second Sino-Japanese War]], the Governor of Osaka established the Gokoku Shrine Construction Support Association in 1938 (Shōwa 13). The site, a low-lying area southwest of Suminoe Park, was filled in by about 360,000 volunteers over two years. The shrine was designated a Gokoku Shrine by the Interior Minister and was enshrined on May 4, 1940 (Shōwa 15), initially in temporary buildings due to shortages of materials and labor caused by the war.
After World War II, the shrine was renamed "Naniwa-no-Miya" and enshrined Emperor Nintoku in response to the GHQ’s Shinto Directive. The shrine was restored to its original name, Osaka Gokoku Shrine, following the signing of the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952 (Shōwa 27).
In 1960 (Shōwa 35), a new shrine building was constructed, and the shrine was officially moved to its current location in 1963 (Shōwa 38). The shrine was visited by Emperor Shōwa and Empress Kōjun on July 15, 1970, and by the Crown Prince and Princess (now Emperor Emeritus Akihito and Empress Emerita Michiko) on May 31, 1978.
A statue honoring special attack forces was erected on October 24, 2009 (Heisei 21).
== Deities == * Main deity - Over 105,000 war dead from Osaka Prefecture (exactly 105,665)
== Clergy and Activities == * Former chief priest Chūmaro Yanagisawa served as the Osaka executive chairman of [[Nippon Kaigi]] and the Osaka prefectural head of the [[Shinto Seiji Renmei]]. He passed away on March 31, 2018. * Masachika Fujie became chief priest in 2021 (Reiwa 3). He organized a memorial service for former Prime Minister [[Shinzo Abe]] following his assassination.
== Grounds == * main hall - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Heiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Haiden - Built in 1963 (Shōwa 38) * Hōanden - Enshrines [[Emperor Nintoku]] and Admiral [[Tōgō Heihachirō]] * Sorei-sha "Homare-no-Miya" * festival Warehouses * Administrative Offices * Suminoe Hall * Ritual Hall (Former Gokoku Dojo) * Garden * Memorials for various military units
== Access == * Near [[Suminoe Koen Station]] on the [[Osaka Metro Yotsubashi Line]] and [[Osaka Metro Nanko Port Town Line|New Tram]]

鳥居と拝殿 |所在地 = [[大阪府]][[大阪市]][[住之江区]][[南加賀屋]]1丁目1-77 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|36|36.5|N|135|28|26.1|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = 大阪府出身ならびに縁故の殉国の[[英霊]]10万5千余[[柱]] |社格 = [[内務大臣 (日本)|内務大臣]]指定護国神社・[[別表神社]] |創建 = [[1940年]]（[[昭和]]15年） |例祭 = [[5月20日]]（春季）・[[10月20日]]（秋季） |地図={{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=大阪護國神社|coordinates= {{coord2|34|36|36.5|N|135|28|26.1|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|36|36.5|N|135|28|26.1|E}}}} }} '''大阪護國神社'''（おおさかごこくじんじゃ）は、[[大阪市]][[住之江区]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）。大阪府出身ならびに縁故の殉国の[[英霊]]10万5千余[[柱]]を祀っており、祭神の柱数は護国神社では[[沖縄県護国神社]]、[[福岡縣護國神社]]に次いで3番目に多い。現在は[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]。 [[住之江公園]]の南西に鎮座し、[[新なにわ筋]]を挟んで[[住之江競艇場]]に隣接している。[[住之江通]]に面した正面[[鳥居]]は大阪府で最大の鳥居である。 == 歴史 == 大阪府では、[[1871年]]（[[明治]]4年）[[4月10日]]に創設された[[真田山陸軍墓地]]の敷地内に[[招魂社]]が創建されたのが最初である。しかし、招魂祭は、[[西南戦争]]後は[[大日本帝国陸軍|陸軍]]の拠点である[[大坂城]]内で、[[1883年]]（明治16年）以降は[[中之島 (大阪府)|中之島]]に建立された明治紀念標{{efn|明治紀念標は[[1902年]]（明治35年）に[[大手前]]の大阪[[偕行社]]敷地内に移設され、[[1943年]]（[[昭和]]18年）に供出されており、現存しない。}}前で、[[1900年]]（明治33年）以降は城東練兵場{{efn|現在の大阪市[[城東区]][[森之宮]]。[[大阪砲兵工廠]]の拡張に伴い、[[1940年]]（昭和15年）に[[中河内郡]][[盾津|盾津町]]へ移転した。}}で行われていた。なお、正確な時期は不明であるが、真田山の招魂社は[[1918年]]（[[大正]]7年）までには廃絶されている。 招魂祭が長らく社前で行われなくなっていたことや、[[支那事変]]（[[日中戦争]]）勃発後に[[英霊]]崇敬の機運が高まりを見せたことから、[[1938年]]（[[昭和]]13年）に大阪府[[知事]]が奉賛会長として護国神社造営奉賛会を結成し、創建予定地である[[住之江公園]]の南西の低湿地を、府民のべ約36万人が勤労奉仕で約2年かけて埋め立てて、[[内務大臣 (日本)|内務大臣]]指定護國神社として創建された{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.osakagokoku.or.jp/publics/index/80/ |title=御祭神・創建・沿革 |publisher=大阪護國神社 |accessdate=2019-01-07}}。[[1940年]]（昭和15年）[[5月4日]]に鎮座祭が行われたが、人材・資材不足のため正式な社殿の建築をすることができず、仮社殿での鎮座であった。その後に正式な社殿の造営を行う予定であったが、[[大東亜戦争]]の激化や敗戦後の混乱により着工出来ないままであった。 戦後、[[連合国軍最高司令官総司令部|GHQ]]による[[神道指令]]の影響下における存続対策として[[仁徳天皇]]を祀り、「浪速宮（なにわぐう）」と称していた{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.osakagokoku.or.jp/publics/index/7/ |title=ご由緒 |publisher=大阪護國神社 |accessdate=2019-01-07}}。また、[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]に加列されている。 社名は、[[サンフランシスコ講和条約]]締結後の[[1952年]]（昭和27年）に大阪護國神社に復称している{{R|ご由緒}}。こうした経緯から奉安殿に[[仁徳天皇]]を、その相殿に[[東郷平八郎]]を遺髪と共に祀るようになった。 [[1960年]]（昭和35年）に造営奉賛会が結成され、[[1963年]]（昭和38年）春に社殿が竣工し、[[5月29日]]に遷座祭が行われた{{R|沿革}}。[[1970年]]（昭和45年）[[7月15日]]に[[昭和天皇]]と[[香淳皇后]]が親拝され、[[1978年]]（昭和53年）[[5月31日]]には[[皇太子]]・[[皇太子妃]]（現：[[明仁]][[上皇 (天皇退位特例法)|上皇]]・[[上皇后美智子]]）が参拝されている。 [[2009年]]（[[平成]]21年）[[10月24日]]に[[特攻勇士の像]]が建立された。 == 祭神 == * 主祭神 - 大阪府出身ならびに縁故の殉国の[[英霊]]10万5千余[[柱]]（正確には105,665柱）[https://spicomi.net/media/articles/3176 大阪護國神社の徹底取材レポ！ご利益・御朱印・お守り] == 神官・諸活動 == * 元宮司の柳澤忠麿は[[日本会議]]大阪運営委員長、[[神道政治連盟]]大阪府本部長を務めていた{{cite web | url=http://shinseiren-kyoto.jp/wp/wp-content/uploads/2023/06/seisei35.pdf | title=『清政』35号 | publisher=神道政治連盟京都府本部 | date=2003-12-16 | accessdate=2024-3-4 }}。2018年（平成30年）3月31日、柳澤は死去した{{cite web | url=http://kaireikai.main.jp/kokubetsu2018.04.03.htm | title=故　柳澤忠麿　海励会会長 通夜式・告別式　(2018年4月2、3日) | publisher=海上自衛隊を励ます会 | date= | accessdate=2024-3-4 }}。 * 2018年（平成30年）、柳澤の死去に伴い、日本会議大阪南河内支部長の南坊城充興が宮司に就任した{{cite web | url=https://www.nipponkaigi.osaka/?p=3079 | title=日本の息吹　［大阪版］　～ 一人がひとりを！ ～　　第１７8号　平成26年9月号 | publisher=日本会議大阪 | date=2014-8-25 | accessdate=2024-3-4 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.nipponkaigi.osaka/?p=4825 | title=日本の息吹［大阪版］～一人がひとりを！～ 第22３号 平成３０年１０月号 | publisher=日本会議大阪 | date=2018-9-24 | accessdate=2024-3-4 }}。 * 2021年（令和3年）4月、藤江正鎮が宮司に就任。2022年（令和4年）9月中旬、共同通信やNHKなど8つの報道機関が実施した[[故安倍晋三国葬儀|安倍晋三元首相の国葬]]に関する世論調査・アンケート調査ではいずれも「反対」が「賛成」を上回った{{Cite web|和書|title=世論調査（共同通信データ） |publisher=[[東京新聞]] |date=2022-09-22 |url=https://www.tokyo-np.co.jp/digican/shuin2021_yoron?sjkd_page=cont_KA613b0d7b468bd_KA632bf2819c8e9 |access-date=2022-09-24}}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nhk.or.jp/politics/articles/lastweek/89020.html |title=国葬「評価しない」57％ 旧統一教会への対応は？ NHK世論調査 |access-date=2022-09-30 |publisher=日本放送協会 |date=2022-09-13 |website=NHK政治マガジン |language=ja}}{{Cite news|title=安倍元首相の国葬賛否、反対56%　8月比で賛成減る　朝日世論調査|newspaper=朝日新聞デジタル|date=2022-09-12|url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ9D3GNCQ9DUZPS002.html|access-date=2022-09-16|publisher=[[朝日新聞社]]}}。これを受けて藤江は[[自由民主党 (日本)|自由民主党]]大阪府連などに問い合わせ、府内に弔意を示す場がないことを知った。9月26日、大阪護國神社藤江は公式Twitterに献花台を翌日に置くことを通知。Twitterで寄せられた「いいね」は2万件を超えた。同月27日、藤江は産経新聞の取材に応じ、「国葬反対の声ばかりが取り上げられているが、『あれだけ功績があるのに』という憤りがあった。（多くの弔問に）安倍さんが喜んでくれているとうれしい」と述べた{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.yomiuri.co.jp/national/20220927-OYT1T50157/|title=『大阪護国神社にも献花台、多くの人が花手向ける…涙流す人も』|publisher=讀賣新聞オンライン|date=2022-09-27 |accessdate=2023-02-11}}{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.iza.ne.jp/article/20220927-TWSYXKZLJRK35GW55ILMMXFUAM/|title=『大阪護国神社に献花台「感謝伝えたい」ツイッターで反響も』|publisher=産経新聞オンライン|date=2022-09-27 |accessdate=2023-02-11}}{{cite web | author= | url=https://www.sankei.com/article/20230328-LNLWIY45RJIKZDSJPRFEVMJUJE/ | title=【浪速風】暴力で失った無念 | publisher=産経新聞 | date=2023-3-28 | accessdate=2024-3-4 }}。 * 2023年（令和5年）3月26日、板垣退助先生顕彰会が主催する「安倍晋三元総理を偲ぶ慰霊祭」の会場となった。 == 境内 == * 本殿 - [[1963年]]（[[昭和]]38年）春建立。 * 幣殿 - 1963年（昭和38年）春建立。[[神鏡]]は戦前の研磨技術で作ることが出来た最大のものとされており、当時三面作られ、[[東京都]][[渋谷区]]・[[明治神宮]]、[[兵庫県]][[神戸市]]・[[湊川神社]]と当社の三社に奉納されたと伝わっている。 * 拝殿 - 1963年（昭和38年）春建立。 * 奉安殿 - 祭神：[[仁徳天皇]]、[[東郷平八郎]][[元帥]] * 祖霊社「ほまれの宮」 * 祭器庫 * 社務所 * 住之江会館 * 儀式殿（元護國道場） * 庭園 * [[サイパン島]]戦没者慰霊碑 * [[歩兵第217連隊|歩兵第二百十七連隊]]之碑 * [[野砲兵第26連隊|野砲兵第二十六連隊]] つわもの之碑 * [[大日本帝国海軍|海軍]]第一期 飛行専修予備生徒慰霊碑 * 独立輜重兵 第五十四大隊慰霊碑 * [[歩兵第216連隊|歩兵第二百十六連隊]]鎮魂碑 * 香8114部隊（独立歩兵第六十七大隊） 殉國の英霊 このところに鎮まる之碑 * [[歩兵第108連隊|歩兵第百八連隊]] つわもの之霊 ここに鎮まる之碑 * [[歩兵第79連隊|歩兵第七十九連隊]] 戦友はここに眠る之碑 * [[信太山演習場|信太山]][[野砲兵第4連隊|砲四]]会 鎮魂之碑 * 関西甲飛会 [[海軍飛行予科練習生|予科練]]貴様と俺と翼之碑 * [[第34師団 (日本軍)|第三十四師団]]通信隊 鎮魂之碑 * [[騎兵連隊|騎]][[捜索連隊|捜]]会 鎮魂之碑 * 海軍関係戦没者慰霊之碑 * [[第68師団 (日本軍)|第六十八師団]]（檜会） 慰霊碑 * [[歩兵第14連隊|歩兵第十四連隊]]之慰霊碑 * 平和の礎 [[歩兵第8連隊|歩兵第八連隊]]之慰霊碑 * [[歩兵第37連隊|歩兵第三十七連隊]]慰霊碑 * [[騎兵連隊|騎兵第四連隊]]慰霊碑 * 海軍[[大尉]] 粟井俊夫之碑 * 皇風洽六合 明徳侔太陽之碑（海軍[[大将]][[塩沢幸一]]題） * 母に感謝の像 * [[特攻勇士の像]] - [[2009年]]（[[平成]]21年）10月24日造。 * [[安倍晋三]]元総理を偲ぶ[[向日葵]]の栽培 - [[2023年]]（[[令和]]5年）造{{efn|[[2022年]]（[[令和]]4年）9月に行われた[[安倍晋三]]元総理を偲ぶ「[[国葬]]」において寄せられた献花を堆肥として栽培された[[向日葵]]。（境内の説明板による）}}。 == 交通 == * [[Osaka Metro四つ橋線]]・[[Osaka Metro南港ポートタウン線|ニュートラム]] [[住之江公園駅]]下車すぐ ==
